answer: (a) The Jews. (b) Because 40 was the highest number of stripes allowed to be inflicted by their law, and, for fear of exceeding that number, they gave "40 save 1."

subject: General Information
question: What was the "hot-water war" in American history?
answer: It occurred in 1799, and the field of battle was Pennsylvania. Immediately after the suppression of the "Whisky Rebellion," the national government attempted to collect a direct tax on houses. When the officers came to make the necessary measurements, the women resisted them, and drove them away by deluging them with hot water. Hence the name. The disturbance came near developing seriously. One Fries, for making a rescue of parties resisting the collectors of the tax, was convicted of treason and condemned to death. He was pardoned by President Adams. The obnoxious tax was repealed, two or three years later, during President Jefferson's administration.

subject: Arithmetic
question: A railroad train is going at the rate of 60 miles an hour. How many feet does it go in 1½ minutes?
answer: 60 miles an hour=1 mile a minute; then in 1½ minutes train will travel 1½ times 1 mile, or 1½ miles. 1 mile =5,280 ft. 1½ miles=5,280 X 3 =7,920 ft. 2

subject: Arithmetic
question: Find the length of the side of a square field which contains 100 acres.
answer: Area=length X width. 100 a.=area. 100 a.=16,000 sq. rd. 16,000 sq. rd.=length X width. Since length and width are equal in a square, 16,000 sq. rd.=length², or width². √16,000=length, or width, or 126.5 rds. nearly.

subject: Arithmetic
question: Each shovelful of earth thrown out by a laborer excavating a cellar averages two-thirds of a cubic foot of earth. At that rate, how many shovelsful will be required to fill a wagon which holds a cubic yard of earth?
answer: Cu. yd.=27 cu. ft. 27 cu. ft.÷⅔=shovelfuls. 40½=shovelfuls.

subject: Arithmetic
question: Three counties combined are required to furnish the national army a regiment of soldiers—1,000 men. The populations of the counties are 65,000, 48,500, and 76,200, respectively. How many men must each county furnish?
answer: The figures given would give us a fraction of a man for each county. Take instead the number of troops to be furnished as 1,897, instead of 1,000; then the counties would furnish quotas as follows: 65000 189700 of 1,897=650. 48500 189700 of 1,897=485. 76200 189700 of 1,897=762.

subject: Arithmetic
question: The length of the school term in a certain district is 180 days, or 1,080 hours. One of the pupils was absent during the term 35 days, and lost, by tardiness, 40 hours. What percentage of the whole time did he lose?
answer: 6 hrs. in school day. 6 X 35, or 210 hrs., in 35 school days. 210 + 40=250 lost hrs. 250 is 23 7/27 % of 1,080 hrs.

subject: Language and Grammar
question: Make sentences in which the following words are used correctly: Tangible, adept, tenacious, respectively, indigenous, ingenuous, ingenious, perfidious, pernicious, cynosure.
answer: That which can be touched is tangible. He is an adept in flattery. The Scotch are a tenacious people. He spoke of them as a, b, and c, respectively. Tobacco is indigenous to America. A child is more ingenuous than an adult. The inventor is ingenious. He is a perfidious man. That doctrine is pernicious. The man in public life is the cynosure of all eyes.

subject: Language and Grammar
question: Give two good reasons why slang should never be used.
answer: Slang is vulgar, and corrupts the language as well as the individual. It is difficult to understand. Language should be clear, and susceptible of but one meaning.

subject: Language and Grammar
question: Give at least five rules which you should follow in letter writing.
answer: (a) Write the full address plainly at the beginning of the letter. (b) Be brief and clear. (c) Make a paragraph for the expression of each thought. (d) Be sure that no words are misspelled. (e) See that your punctuation assists in interpreting your thought. (f) Write your address in full at the close. The first and last directions are to secure either the safe delivery or return of the letter should it miscarry.

subject: Language and Grammar
question: Give an example of epigram, irony, hyperbole, metaphor, metonymy.
answer: "A little learning is a dangerous thing."—Pope. "A nice man is a man of nasty ideas."—Swift. (He intended the reverse.) "Is she not more than painting can express?"—Rowe. "He was not merely a chip off the old block, he was the old block itself."—Burke. "Martin, if dirt was trumps, what hands you would hold."—Charles Lamb.

subject: Language and Grammar
question: Give three English words derived from the Celtic, three from the Dutch, three from the French, three from the Italian, three from the Spanish.
answer: Celtic—clan, brogue, whisky. Dutch—patroon, boss, stoop. French—levee, crevasse, bayou. Italian—macaroni, vermicelli, pantaloon. Spanish—ranche, cañon, stampede.

subject: Geography
question: Name a country in which each of the following-named animals is used as a beast of burden: The llama, yak, camel, elephant, reindeer, dog.
answer: Llama, Peru; yak, India; camel, Egypt; elephant, India; reindeer, Lapland; dog, Greenland.

subject: Geography
question: What causes the great difference between the climate of the eastern and western coasts of the United States?
answer: The cold currents of the north Atlantic flow near the New England coast, while the return warm current of the Japanese stream flows near the western coast.

subject: Geography
question: Define the following words: Erosion, fauna, flora, snow line, ethnography.
answer: Erosion—the wearing away of the earth by the action of water, air, or other agencies. Fauna—the animal life of a country is its fauna. Flora—the name applied to the vegetable life of a section of the earth. Snow line—the lowest altitude at which snow falls and lies on the mountain side the year around. Ethnography—the branch of knowledge which deals with the characteristics of the human family, furnishing the details for ethnology.

subject: Geography
question: Name the countries in which the franc is current; the gulden; the milreis; the mark; the piastre.
answer: Franc—France, Belgium, and Switzerland. Gulden—Holland. Milreis—Portugal. Mark—Germany. Piastre—Spain, Turkey, and Egypt.

subject: Geography
question: Name the nations to which the following-named places belong: New Caledonia, Faroe Isles, Azores, New Guinea, Caroline islands.
answer: New Caledonia, France; Faroe Isles, Denmark; Azores, Portugal; New Guinea, Germany, The Netherlands, and Great Britain; Caroline islands, Spain.

subject: United States History
question: What was the tenure-of-office act?
answer: It was an act passed during the conflict between Congress and President Johnson. It provided that no officer for whose appointment the consent of the senate was needful could be removed without the consent of the senate.

subject: United States History
question: What was Morgan's raid?
answer: A cavalry raid made by the rebel general John Morgan during the rebellion, in the year 1863, through Kentucky and Ohio.

subject: United States History
question: When was the department of the interior established? What is its work?
answer: In 1849. Has charge of internal affairs, and has under its direction more interests than has any other department, including public lands, patent office, pensions, Indians, census, and education.

subject: United States History
question: What is meant in our history as "the right of expatriation"?
answer: The right of a man to abjure his allegiance to one country and become a citizen of another country.

subject: United States History
question: Under whose administration was the interstate-commerce act passed? the Washington monument completed? standard time adopted? the temperance crusade? the "salary grab"?
answer: (a) President Cleveland's first administration. (b, c) President Arthur's. (d, e) President Grant's second administration.

subject: General Information
question: How fast do glaciers move?
answer: But a few inches in a day. Some do not exceed a few inches in a month.

subject: General Information
question: What made each of the following-named persons celebrated: Haydn, Charles Wolfe, Rouget de Lisle, Comenius, James Wilson?
answer: Haydn—musical composer. Charles Wolfe wrote "The Burial of Sir John Moore. De Lisle wrote the "Marseillaise." Comenius—author of the first pictorial schoolbook. James Wilson—American jurist and signer of the declaration of independence, and one of the most prominent framers of the United States constitution.

subject: General Information
question: What is a "blockade" in war?
answer: The investment of a port by ships, preventing ingress or egress and the reception of supplies.

subject: General Information
question: Give the authors of the expressions, "Knowledge is power;" "God the first garden made, and the first city Cain;" "Not lost, but gone before;" "Men are but children of a larger growth;" "Necessity the mother of invention."
answer: Francis Bacon. Abraham Cowley. Copied from Seneca by both Samuel Rogers, in his "Human Life," and Matthew Henry, in his "Commentaries." John Dryden. The same expression occurs in Franck's "Northern Memoirs," written in 1658 and printed in 1694, Wycherly's "Love in a Wood," 1672, and Farquhar's "Twin Rivals," 1705.

subject: General Information
question: Name the books in which the following names represent characters: Ichabod Crane, Smike, Portia, Dominie Sampson, Doctor Primrose, Miriam, Alfred Jingle, Becky Sharp, Prospero.
answer: Ichabod Crane, "The Legend of Sleepy Hollow. Smike, "Nicholas Nickleby." Portia, "The Merchant of Venice." Dominie Sampson, "Guy Mannering." Doctor Primrose, "The Vicar of Wakefield. Miriam, "The Marble Faun." Alfred Jingle, "The Pickwick Papers." Becky Sharp, "Vanity Fair." Prospero, "The Tempest."

subject: Arithmetic
question: Express the ratio of 2 to 3 in two different ways.

subject: Arithmetic
question: I have a triangular field 6 rods by 7 rods by 9 rods. Find the area, and state the rule.
answer: 6+7+9=22. 22 ÷ 2=11. 11—6=5; 11—7=4; 11—9=2. √5×4×2×11=√440, or 20.97 square rods. When the three sides of a triangle are given, the area is found by taking the square root of the continued product of the half sum of all the sides by the difference between that half sum and each of the respective sides.

subject: Arithmetic
question: Which is the larger, a circle having a diameter of 3 feet, or a square with a side of 3 feet? How much larger?
answer: Area of square = 3² = 9 sq. feet. Area of circle = 3.1416 × (3/2)² = 7.0686, nearly, sq. feet. The square is larger, by 1.9314 square feet.

subject: Arithmetic
question: Compare the cubical contents of a cylinder and a cone having heights and diameters equal.
answer: A cone is one-third of a cylinder having the same base and height.

subject: Arithmetic
question: Indicate 4 raised to the fifth power. Indicate the fifth root of 4 to be extracted.

subject: Language and Grammar
question: Correct the following: Come and see me. What did you do it for? Is that the gent? She is our washer lady. I reckon it will rain.
answer: Come to see me. Why did you do it? Is that the gentleman? She is our washerwoman. I suppose (or think) it will rain.

subject: Language and Grammar
question: Illustrate the proper use of may and can.
answer: May I go? I can do it.

subject: Language and Grammar
question: When should parenthesis marks be used?
answer: Whenever an expression is introduced for explanation or amplification in the midst of a sentence, the marks indicate that the main issue is suspended for the introduction of the subordinate or explanatory thought.

subject: Language and Grammar
question: Classify subordinate conjunctions, giving an example of each class.
answer: They are classified according to the office of the subordinate. Adjective: "I looked for a place where I could climb," etc. Substantive: "I did not doubt that I was done." Adverbial: "He went on, while I stumbled," etc.

subject: Language and Grammar
question: What is meant by the agreement of words?
answer: When two words bear certain grammatical relations one to the other, they are said to agree. Thus, substantives must agree with their verbs in number and person by having the same person and number as do the verbs. —

subject: Geography
question: What is an avalanche? Mention localities where avalanches are common.
answer: Accumulated snow which slips down the slopes of high mountains with resistless force. Switzerland and other countries bordering on the Alps.

subject: Geography
question: What two monarchies of Europe are absolute?
answer: Russia and Turkey.

subject: Geography
question: Give the approximate population of the world; of the United States; of New York state; of Kansas.

subject: Geography
question: Arrange the following-named cities in the order of population, beginning with the largest: Berlin, Pekin, London, Canton, Vienna, New York, Calcutta, Chicago, and Paris.
answer: London, Paris, Pekin, Canton, New York, Berlin, Vienna, Chicago, Calcutta.

subject: Geography
question: Write the names of the states admitted to the union since 1875, giving the capital of each.
answer: Colorado, capital, Denver; North Dakota, capital, Bismarck; South Dakota, capital, Pierre; Montana, capital, Helena; Washington, capital, Olympia; Idaho, capital, Boise City; Wyoming, capital, Cheyenne.

subject: United States History
question: What were the chief points in the compromise of 1850?
answer: California to be admitted as a free state; Utah (including Nevada) and New Mexico (including Arizona) to be organized without reference to slavery; the slave trade to be abolished in the District of Columbia; the passing of a more stringent fugitive slave law.

subject: United States History
question: Who were the “anti-Nebraska” men?
answer: When, in 1854, the Kansas-Nebraska bill was passed, virtually repealing the Missouri compromise, all northern men who were opposed to the further extension of slavery united under the name of anti-Nebraska men. This was the origin of the Republican party.

subject: United States History
question: What was the estimated cost of the war of the rebellion?
answer: About eight billions of dollars.

subject: United States History
question: What were “wildcat banks,” and when were they prominent in our history?
answer: They were banks which sprang up all over the country during Jackson's last term. These banks issued bills which they were not able to redeem. Bills issued in one state would be refused or heavily discounted in another. When President Jackson ordered that payments for public lands (in which at that time there was a wild speculation) be paid in specie, people flocked to the banks to exchange bills for specie. The results were bank failures and the panic of 1837.

subject: United States History
question: State briefly the provisions of the “presidential succession act” and the “electoral count act.”
answer: (a) It was passed in 1886, and provided that, in case of the death or disability of both the President and vice president, the succession to the presidency shall be in the following order: Secretary of state; of the treasury; of war; attorney general; secretary of the navy; postmaster general; secretary of the interior. The department of agriculture had not been instituted at that time. It is a later creation. It is provided that the member who thus becomes President shall be known as "acting President," and that he shall serve until the disability is removed or until a new President is elected. (6) It was passed in 1887. It provides for the settlement of disputes relative to the validity of the electoral vote from any state by the tribunals of that state.

subject: General Information
question: What is the origin of the phrase “higher law”?
answer: It was uttered by William H. Seward in a speech delivered on March 11, 1850.

subject: General Information
question: Who was the greatest literary forger that ever lived?
answer: Alcibiades Simonides, born on the island of Cyrene, 1818; died 1890. He published forgeries of Homer's writings, the Assyrian tablets, and the philosophies of the eastern and western Roman empires.

subject: General Information
question: What are the correct names for the following nom de plumes? Ik Marvel, Charles Egbert Craddock, Boz, Junius, Waverly.
answer: Donald Mitchell, Miss Murfree, Charles Dickens, probably Sir Philip Francis, Sir Walter Scott.

subject: General Information
question: Give the title of the best work, in your opinion, of Byron, Shelley, Goldsmith, Hugo, Goethe, Cervantes, Virgil, Hawthorne, Bryant, and Burns.
answer: Childe Harold, The Cloud or the Skylark, The Vicar of Wakefield, Les Miserables, Faust, Don Quixote, Æneid, The Scarlet Letter, Thanatopsis, The Cotter's Saturday Night.

subject: General Information
question: Name four good histories of the United States, and give the period covered by each.
answer: Bancroft's brings our history down to the formation of the constitution. Von Holst's Constitutional History of the United States gives our constitutional history from its origin. Hildreth's History (federalist) brings our history down to 1820. Schouler's History (democratic) comes down to the Mexican war. McMaster's History (republican) covers the period from the revolution to the civil war.

subject: Arithmetic
question: A railway train leaves Chicago for Kansas City at 6 P. M., and goes at the rate of 3,080 feet per minute. Another train on the same line leaves Kansas City for Chicago at 5 P. M., and goes at the rate of 3,520 feet per minute. If the distance from Chicago to Kansas City is 490 miles, where will the trains pass.
answer: At midnight, 210 miles from Chicago and 280 miles from Kansas City.

subject: Arithmetic
question: Which will cost the more and how much: to fence a tract of land 50 rods square, or a tract 125 rods long and 20 rods wide? supposing the fencing to cost—cents per rod. Which is the larger field?
answer: The cost of fencing the square would be to the cost of fencing the parallelogram as 1 : 1.45. Thus, supposing the cost to be 50 cents per rod, the expense of fencing the square would be $100, and that of the parallelogram, $145.

subject: Arithmetic
question: On a farm containing 160 acres in the form of a square a ravine averaging 60 rods long and 6 rods wide cannot be cultivated; a public road running along two sides of the farm takes off a width of two rods; and there is a tract of timber land averaging 40 rods long and 15 rods wide. Deducting the number of acres which cannot be cultivated, how much is left?
answer: The total area of the farm = 25,600 square rods. (a) Supposing the road to run along parallel sides: Ravine's area = 60 X 6 = 360 sq. rods. Road's area = 160 X 2 X 2 = 640 " Timber land's area = 40 X 15 = 600 " Area of untillable land = 1,600 sq. rods. 25,600 sq. rods — 1,600 sq. rods = 24,000 sq. rods, or 150 acres. (b) Supposing the road to run along adjacent sides, the amount of untillable land would be 4 sq. rods less, and the tillable land would be 25,600 sq. rods — 1,596 sq. rods = 24,004 sq. rods, or 150 4/16 acres.

subject: Arithmetic
question: A telephone pole is 40 feet high. From the top of a post 10 feet high, set perpendicularly at a distance of 20 feet from the foot of the pole, a wire is stretched to the top of the pole. How long is the wire?
answer: The upper 30 feet of the telephone pole would constitute the perpendicular of a right-angled triangle, of which the distance from the top of the post to the telephone pole (20 ft.) would be the base, and the wire the hypotenuse. √20² + 30² = √1300 = 36.05+ feet, length of the wire.

subject: Arithmetic
question: In a two-thirds pitch roof, what is the length of the rafters, if the building is 24 feet wide?
answer: The height of the pitch is ⅔ of 24 feet = 16 feet, the distance from the vertex of the roof to the square. This is the altitude of a right-angled triangle whose base is half the width of the building (12 ft.), and whose hypotenuse is the length of the rafter. √16² + 12² = √400 = 20 feet, length of rafter.

subject: Language and Grammar
question: Make sentences in which the following words are used correctly: Nefarious, incongruous, discrepancies, exemplary, precedent (verb), precedent (noun), contumely, truculent, contest (verb), contest (noun).
answer: It is my purpose to defeat his nefarious schemes. That man's dress is incongruous. There are several discrepancies in the statement made by the witness. He is a man of exemplary character. Precedent is not a verb, but may be a noun or adjective; for example: "Precedent injury," "There is no precedent for this action." He treated his visitor with contumely. The truculent natives of Uganda. I will contest the election. There will be a contest between the two colleges.

subject: Language and Grammar
question: What words are opposite in meaning to the following: Ideal, fact, infinite, proud, zenith, frigid, constant, theist, condemn, progression.
answer: Realistic, fiction, finite, humble, nadir, torrid, fickle, atheist, praise, retrogression.

subject: Language and Grammar
question: Write sentences containing the word "that" as a noun, pronoun, adjective, conjunction.
answer: That is a relative pronoun. That is the dog that bit me. That man is a good citizen. He will say that you may go.

subject: Language and Grammar
question: What may be the antecedent of a relative pronoun?
answer: Any substantive, whether word, phrase, or clause.

subject: Language and Grammar
question: Correct the following, and give reasons: "I expect he is sick." "They presented him with a gold-headed cane." "He enthused the meeting." "He died with a fever." "Yours, &c." (at close of a letter).
answer: (a) I suppose he is sick. Expect means to look for, to look forward to, or to anticipate; hence is wrongly used in the sentence given. (b) They presented him a gold-headed cane. With is superfluous. (c) Enthused is slang. The sentence could be written in various ways, as: He aroused a great deal of enthusiasm in the meeting. (d) He died of a fever. The wrong preposition is used. (e) &c. is a disrespectful way of closing a letter, and is inexcusable.

subject: Geography
question: Has Lake Michigan any tide? If so, how much?
answer: Yes; at Chicago. About three inches.

subject: Geography
question: Define field ice, floe, pack ice, drift ice.
answer: (a) Ice covering large surfaces. (b) A large, floating mass of ice. (c) Pieces of broken ice closely packed together. (d) Ice floating about.

subject: Geography
question: How do you account for the salt deposits in New York, Ontario, and Michigan?
answer: Lakes Huron and Ontario were at a remote period part of an arm of the sea. The Mohawk and Hudson valleys formed the rest of the arm.

subject: Geography
question: Name the five countries of the world which have the largest coal areas.
answer: China, United States, Canada, India, Russia.

subject: Geography
question: What is the difference between anthracite and bituminous coal? Name five of the states in which coal is found in large quantities.
answer: (a) Anthracite coal is hard and compact, has a high luster, has but little bitumen; hence its flame is almost non-luminous. Bituminous coal contains much bitumen—a black, tarry substance—burns with a yellow, smoky flame, and is soft. (b) Pennsylvania, Missouri, Alabama, Illinois, Kansas.

subject: United States History
question: Name a vice president of the United States who died before he could begin to perform the duties of his office.
answer: William Rufus King. He was elected in 1852.

subject: United States History
question: Name an American who was a poet, an essay-ist, and a diplomatist; one who was an essayist, a scientific investigator, and a statesman; two who were historians and diplomatists; one who was a poet and a diplomatist.
answer: (a) James Russell Lowell. (b) Benjamin Franklin. (c) John Lothrop Motley and George Bancroft. (d) Bayard Taylor.

subject: United States History
question: Name three celebrated persons from each of the following-named states, and name one important event or work with which each person named was connected: Pennsylvania, Indiana, Massachusetts, Missouri, Virginia.
answer: Pennsylvania: Benjamin Franklin, scientist and diplomat; author of “Poor Richard’s Almanac.” Stephen Girard, founder of Girard College. Albert Gallatin, financier, second secretary of the treasury of the United States. Indiana: Oliver P. Morton, statesman; great war governor during the rebellion. Lew Wallace, author of “The Fair God” and “Ben Hur.” Benjamin Harrison, statesman, President of the United States. Massachusetts: Daniel Webster, statesman, most distinguished American orator; “Oration against Hayne.” Horace Mann, greatest American educator; Massachusetts educational department. Nathaniel Hawthorne, greatest American novelist; "Scarlet Letter." Missouri: Thomas H. Benton, statesman; wrote "Thirty Years in the United States Senate." James B. Eads, inventor and constructive engineer; built the great Mississippi river bridge at St. Louis, the jetties, etc. B. Gratz Brown, statesman; candidate on the liberal republican ticket for vice president, with Horace Greeley for President. Virginia: George Washington, general and statesman, commander of continental armies, and first President of the United States. James Madison, statesman, President of the United States during the war of 1812; author of a number of the Federalist papers. Thomas Jefferson, statesman, President of the United States; wrote declaration of independence and bought Louisiana.

subject: United States History
question: Name the political parties which nominated the following-named candidates for President of the United States: James Birney, John Quincy Adams, Peter Cooper, Lewis Cass, Winfield Scott, John Parker Hale, Horace Greeley.
answer: Liberty party; national republican; independent; democratic; whig; liberal republican.

subject: United States History
question: Name a President of the United States who was censured by the senate; another who was cen-sured by the house. State briefly the reasons for the action in each case.
answer: On March 28, 1834, the United States senate censured President Jackson for exercising powers not conferred upon him by the constitution when he removed the deposits from the United States Bank. In 1842, a report censuring President Tyler for vetoing a tariff bill was passed by the house of representatives.

subject: General Information
question: Against what evils were the following-named books a protest: Nicholas Nickleby, Don Quixote, Les Miserables, Little Dorrit, Uncle Tom's Cabin?
answer: (a) The brutalities which prevailed in private schools. (b) Knight-errantry. (c) The severity of French criminal law. (d) Debtors' prisons and "red tape" in government offices. (e) Slavery.

subject: General Information
question: What book or other literary work does each of the following names suggest to you: Dromio, Hopeful, Trinculo, Currer Bell, Wilhelm Meister, Thoreau, Cuttle, Motley, Jekyll, Gulliver?
answer: (a) Comedy of Errors. (b) Pilgrim's Progress. (c) The Tempest. (d) Jane Eyre. (e) Book of that name, by Goethe. (f) Walden, or Life in the Woods. (g) Dombey and Son. (h) Rise of the Dutch Republic. (i) The Strange Case of Doctor Jekyll and Mr. Hyde. (j) Gulliver's Travels.

subject: General Information
question: What are meant by the terms "chamber of commerce" and "board of trade"?
answer: They virtually mean the same thing. Chamber of commerce is defined by one authority to be "an assembly of merchants and traders where affairs relating to trade are treated of." Another authority gives this definition: "A society of the principal merchants and traders, who meet to promote the general commerce of the place."

subject: General Information
question: What are cipher dispatches? Give an illustration of system.
answer: Dispatches written in signs made to correspond to letters of the alphabet, or by placing the letters out of their regular order. For instance, let the lower letter represent the upper, thus: a, b, c, d, l, m, n, o, e, f, g, h, i, j, k, l, m, n, o, p, q, r, s, t, u, v, x, y, z. p, q, r, s, t, u, v, x, y, z, a, b, c, d, e, f, g, h, i, j, k. Following this arrangement, if one wished to dispatch, "Hold the fort," the message would be as follows: "Saxo fsp qadf."

subject: General Information
question: State briefly what you know concerning Alsace-Lorraine; its area, boundaries, language, government.
answer: Two provinces ceded by France to Germany at the close of the Franco-German war in 1871. The territory is situated between France and Germany, has an area of 5,580 square miles. German is the prevailing language, but French is spoken by a considerable number of the people. Much of the French population left for France when the provinces were surrendered to Germany.

subject: Arithmetic
question: How many square yards of cloth will be required for a tent in the form of a cone 15 feet in diameter and 18 feet high?
answer: Slant height = √18² + 7½² = 19.5. Half slant height = 9.75 ft. Circumference of base = 15 × 3.1416 = 47.124 ft. 47.124 × 9.75 = 459.459 sq. ft. = 51.051 yards.

subject: Arithmetic
question: How many minutes were there from 10:40 A. M., February 4, 1892, to 1:30 P. M., March 2, 1892?
answer: From 10:40 A. M. to midnight, February 4, 1892 = 13½ hours. From February 4 to 1:30 P. M., March 2, 1892 = 26 days, 13½ hours. Total number of hours from 10:40 A. M., February 4, to 1:30 P. M., March 2, 650½; total number of minutes, 39,050.

subject: Arithmetic
question: The assessed valuation of a certain school district is $9,000. It is allowed by law to levy a tax of 2 mills on each dollar of valuation. The district receives $25 a year from the state school fund. Coal and other incidental expenses amount to $40 a year. How much, if the full rate is levied, will be left for teachers' wages? If the average teacher is worth $40 a month, and the length of the school term should be nine months, how much does the district lack?
answer: We should have written 20 mills instead of 2. $9,000 × .020 = $180. 180 + $25 = 205. 205 - 40 = 165. 165 ÷ 40 = 4⅛. The district lacks 4⅛ months of school, even though the maximum rate of taxation is levied.

subject: Arithmetic
question: The area of the grounds of a schoolhouse is one acre. The schoolhouse is 24 feet long, and 16 feet wide. What percentage of the grounds does the schoolhouse occupy?
answer: 1 A. = 43,560 sq ft. Area occupied by schoolhouse = 384 sq. ft. Percentage of the grounds occupied by the schoolhouse, .0088+.

subject: Arithmetic
question: What percentage in grains is a pound troy of a pound avoirdupois? An ounce troy of an ounce avoirdupois? What percentage in cubic inches is a pint, liquid measure, of a peck, dry measure?

subject: Language and Grammar
question: Make sentences in which the following words are used correctly: Emigration, immigration, nepotism, persuasive, peremptory, predilection, surveillance, suavity, illusive, elusive.
answer: There will be a heavy emigration of negroes from the Southern states to Liberia.—It is hoped that the immigration to KanLoss this year will be very great. In his appointments the comptroller can justly be charged with nepotism. The persuasive powers of that lawyer are very great. In a peremptory manner he told him to leave the room. The judge had a predilection for the prisoner. The police had him under surveillance while he remained in the city. He was a man of much suavity of manners. Life is illusive. He answered me in an elusive manner.

subject: Language and Grammar
question: Write the participles of the following verbs: Fight, sit, set.
answer: Fight: fighting, fought, having fought, being fought, having been fought. Sit: sitting, sat, having sat. Set: setting, set, having set. Being set, having been set.

subject: Language and Grammar
question: What is meant by a double relative? Give example of its use.
answer: The double relative is the pronoun "what." It is so called because it may be separated into "that which." Example: He will do what is right.

subject: Language and Grammar
question: Give the plural form for molasses, mouse, fish, memorandum, politics.
answer: (a) Has no plural form. (b) Mice. (c) Fish or fishes. (d) Memoranda or memorandums. (e) Has no plural form.

subject: Language and Grammar
question: "It rains." "It is he." Why are the foregoing expressions correct? What grammatical rules do they seem to violate? What name is given to such expressions?
answer: (a) Because the immemorial usage of the best English writers and speakers has sanctioned those forms. (b) "It" is singular, and is used to represent the plural noun "elements." "It" is neuter, and is the antecedent of "he" which is masculine. (c) They are called idioms.

subject: Geography
question: Where is Two Ocean pass, and why so named?
answer: In the Yellowstone region. So named because the waters flow in opposite directions—part toward the Atlantic; part toward the Pacific.

subject: Geography
question: What is meant by "The Roaring Forties"?
answer: Below 40 deg. south latitude the anti-trade winds blow more steadily than elsewhere. These winds have been named "The Roaring Forties" by seamen.

subject: Geography
question: Substitute the right name for each of the following: Far Cathay, The Floating City, The Eye of the East, The Queen of the Antilles, Land of the Rising Sun.
answer: China, Bangkok, Damascus, Cuba, Japan.

subject: Geography
question: Name the largest peninsula in the world, tell where it is situated, and give approximately its area.
answer: Arabia. Western Asia. More than one million square miles.

subject: Geography
question: Name the largest inland sheet of water in the world, tell where it is situated, and what may be remarkable about it, and give approximately its area.
answer: Caspian sea. Southeastern Russia, between Europe and Asia. Its waters are salt. 170,000 square miles.

subject: United States History
question: A graduate of Harvard was a delegate to the Continental Congress, 1784-'86; was United States senator; then minister to Great Britain; again United States senator; and, again, minister to Great Britain. Name him.
answer: Rufus King.

subject: United States History
question: A graduate of Princeton was in succession United States senator, minister to Russia, vice president of the United States, minister to Great Britain. Name him.
answer: George Mifflin Dallas.

subject: United States History
question: Give the number of senators and representatives in the Congress of 1790, and the ratio of representation. Give the number and ratio in 1893.
answer: In 1790, number of representatives, 65; ratio of representatives, 30,000. In 1893, number, 356; ratio, 173,901.

subject: United States History
question: After a state is admitted into the Union, when does its star appear on our national flag?
answer: On the 4th of July following the admission of the state.

subject: United States History
question: Beginning with 1800, give the percentage of increase in population in the United States every 10 years, to and including 1890.

subject: General Information
question: What is meant by each of the following words: Autonomy, armistice, oligarchy, bureaucracy?
answer: (a) It originally meant the power of self-government, but the word now is understood to mean the independence in local matters which a state itself, part of a confederation, may have, subject to the national constitution or laws. Thus, the states of the American union, of the German empire and the cantons of Switzerland have autonomy. (b) A temporary suspension of hostilities between two armies by mutual agreement. (c) Gov-ernment in which the power is placed in the hands of a few persons. (d) The exercise of undue influ-ence and authority by the collective bureaus of a government.

subject: General Information
question: What is "filibustering" in parliamentary law?
answer: The tactics by which a minority in a legisla-tive body obstructs obnoxious legislation. These tactics may be in numerous forms, such as making motions to adjourn, rising to points of order, moving to lay on the table, moving to reconsider, long speeches, etc.

subject: General Information
question: Define "funding" and "refunding."
answer: (a) To convert a floating debt into interest-bearing bonds. (b) The renewal of a debt which has been already funded.

subject: General Information
question: Name the countries in Europe which have two legislative chambers, which have but one, and which have no legislature.
answer: (a) Great Britain, France, Spain, Italy, Aus-tria, Germany, Switzerland, Denmark, Holland, Bel-gium, Sweden, and Norway. (b) Greece, Servia, and Bulgaria. (c) Russia and Turkey.

subject: General Information
question: How many times and in what manner has the government of France been changed since 1789?
answer: Twelve times. Republic 1793, directory (gov-ernment by five directors), consulate (government by three consuls), consulate (first consul given extraor-dinary powers), the first empire, the monarchy, first empire restored, monarchy restored, monarchy by election, republic, the second empire, the republic.

subject: Arithmetic
question: My watch gains 55 seconds per day. I put it right at 4 P. M. on Saturday. What time will it be by my watch at 10 A. M. on the following Thursday?
answer: The time from 4 P. M. on Saturday to 10 A. M. on the following Thursday = 4¾ days 55 sec. X 4¾ = 261¼ sec. = 4 min. 17 sec. Hence, the time will be 4 min. 17 sec. after 10 A. M. on Thursday.

subject: Arithmetic
question: I sold the N. E. ¼ of the S. E. ¼ of the N. W. ¼ of a section of land at $12.75 an acre. How much did I get for the land?
answer: The N. E. ¼ of the S. E. ¼ of the N. W. ¼ of a section of land (not fractional) = 10 acres. $12.75 X 10 = $127.50.

subject: Arithmetic
question: If I go diagonally across from the S. E. corner to the N. W. corner of the S. W. ¼ of the S. W. ¼ of a section of land instead of going on the boundary lines, how much do I save in the distance?
answer: If I go on the boundary lines, I must travel 320 rods The diagonal = √160² + 160² = 226.45+. 320 - 226.45+ = 93.55. Hence, I save 93.55 rods in distance.

subject: Arithmetic
question: How many barrels (31½ gallons) will a cubical cistern that is 8 feet deep contain?
answer: 8³ X 1728 = 884736 = number of cubic inches in cistern. 884736 / 231 = 3830+ = number of gals. in the cistern. 3830+ ÷ 31½ = 12+ = number of barrels.

subject: Arithmetic
question: How many hours will there be in the year 1900?
answer: The year 1900 is the last in the century. It is not a leap year because its number is not divisible by 400. Hence, the number of hours = 24 hrs. X 365 = 8,760 hrs.

subject: Language and Grammar
question: Make sentences in which the following words are used correctly: Coterie, naïve, nave, façade, portentous, ominous, accession, tension, retentive, promulgate.
answer: The brilliant coteries of Boston. She told her story in a naïve manner. There is a nave in the cathedral. That building has a beautiful façade. These dark clouds in the west are portentous. His frown is ominous. The accession of Alaska to the United States was of great importance. The tension on his muscles was very painful. He has a retentive memory. He is about to promulgate a decree.

subject: Language and Grammar
question: Correct the following, and give reasons for each correction: “He took up school.” “I do not blame the failure on Jones.” “There is no doubt but what he will go.”
answer: (a) He began the school. Because the other expression is slang. (b) I do not blame Jones for the failure. You cannot blame a failure on anybody. The entire expression is an ungrammatical jumble. (c) There is no doubt but he will go. What is superfluous.

subject: Language and Grammar
question: Define euphemism, euphony, repartee, and give an example of each.
answer: (a) A figure by which a mild or inoffensive word is substituted for a harsh one. Example: He abstracted (stole) a handkerchief from the counter. (b) An easy and a smooth enunciation of sounds. Example: "The wolf's long howl on Onalooska's shore." (c) A smart, ready and witty reply. Example: "What's going on?" said a bore who stopped Douglas Jerrold on the street. "I am," replied Jerrold, passing on.

subject: Language and Grammar
question: What is meant by poetical license? Illustrate.
answer: Poets sometimes violate the rules of syntax, and this practice is called "poetical license." Example: "And in the lowest deep a lower deep." "Where echo walks steep hills among."

subject: Language and Grammar
question: What is blank verse? Give an example.
answer: Measures in poetry in which there are no rhymes, usually each verse ends with an important word. Example: "From morn To noon he fell, from noon to dewy eve, A summer's day; and with the setting sun Dropped from the zenith like a falling star.' —Milton.

subject: Geography
question: Give the meaning of low barometer and high barometer.
answer: When the air is heavy it pushes the mercury in a barometer up; when the air is light, the mercury falls.

subject: Geography
question: Why is it that the water of the ocean does not freeze as readily as fresh water? Is there any difference in composition between the ice on the ocean and the water from which it is formed?
answer: Because the freezing of salt water takes place at a lower temperature than that of fresh water. Salt water freezes at 29 deg. Fah., fresh at 32 deg. Fah.

subject: Geography
question: Name an island on which a celebrated author died; another on which a noted general died; one on which a great general was born; and two which are named in a popular hymn.
answer: Robert Louis Stevenson died on Upolu, one of the Samoan islands. Napoleon died on the island of St. Helena, and he was born on the island of Corsica. Greenland and Ceylon are named in the hymn "From Greenland's Icy Mountains."

subject: Geography
question: What is meant by each of the following words: Boers, Maoris, Papuans, atoll, mosque?
answer: (a) The inhabitants of two republics, the Orange River Free State, and the South African Republic, both in South Africa. (b) The original natives of New Zealand. (c) The natives of the Australasian islands. (d) Islands ring-shaped, and wholly composed of the skeletons of coral polyps, situated in the Pacific ocean. (e) A Mohammedan church.

subject: Geography
question: What and where are pusstas, selvas, steppes, llanos, pampas?
answer: (a) Great plains of Hungary. (b) Selvas (misprinted salvas) are the great forests of the Amazon valley. (c) The great treeless plains of Russia. Also applied to the plains of Patagonia. (d) The plains of the lower Orinoco. (e) The treeless plains of the Argentine Republic.

subject: United States History
question: A certain man was successively pastor of a church, representative in Congress, governor of a state, minister to Great Britain, secretary of state, United States senator, candidate for vice president. Name him and his native state.
answer: Edward Everett.

subject: United States History
question: What is meant by the term "pocket veto"?
answer: Bills passed during the last ten days of a session, if kept by the President until Congress adjourns, do not become laws. Hence, this way of nullifying a bill is called "pocket veto."

subject: United States History
question: What was the "Ostend Manifesto"? and by whom was it issued?
answer: On October 9, 1854, James Buchanan, John Y. Mason, and Pierre Soulé, the American ministers to Great Britain, France, and Spain, met at Ostend, under the direction of President Pierce, and drew up the dispatch to the United States government, now known as the "Ostend Manifesto." The substance of this dispatch was that the sale of Cuba would be as advantageous and honorable to Spain as its purchase would be to the United States, but that if Spain refused to sell it, self-preservation would make it necessary for the United States to wrest it from her.

subject: United States History
question: When was a blockade of the Southern ports proclaimed? When and by whom was the blockade raised?
answer: (a) On April 23, 1861, by President Lincoln. (b) On May 22, 1865, by President Johnson. The blockade in Texas was not raised until a month later.

subject: United States History
question: How much was the debt of the United States in 1860? How much in 1865?

subject: General Information
question: What is the origin of the term "grain," used in troy and avoirdupois weights?
answer: The old English pound was equal to the weight of 7,680 grains of wheat, hence, the name grain. The standard has changed since that time.

subject: General Information
question: Give the origin of each of the following words: Augury, sophistry, auspices, mortgage, blackmail.
answer: (a) The augurs in ancient Rome were a college or board whose duty it was to interpret the signs of approval or disapproval which Jupiter was supposed to send in regard to any public transaction. Hence, our word augury. (b) Derived from a school of philosophy in ancient Greece, the members of which were known as sophists. They were noted for their ingenuity in making the worse appear the better reason. Hence, our word sophistry, meaning a reasoning which is sound in appearance only. (d) Synonym of auguries. (e) From two French words, mort dead, and gage, a pledge, so called because in case of nonpayment of the debt, the land was forever dead, and lost to the mortgagor. (d) A certain rate of money, corn or cattle paid to bands of robbers in the south of Scotland and north of England, which secured to those who paid the protection of the robbers.

subject: General Information
question: Name the author of each of the following quo-tations : "Who does the best his circumstance allows, Does well, acts nobly; angels could do no more." "For just experience tells in every soil That those who think must govern those who toil." "I am a part of all that I have met." "Measure your mind's height by the shade it casts."
answer: (a) Rogers. (b) Goldsmith. (c) Tennyson. (d) Browning.

subject: General Information
question: Whence were the names of the seven days of the week derived?
answer: From the names of Pagan gods. Sunday, Sun's day; Monday, Moon's day; Tuesday, Tuisto or Tuesco (Saxon god); Wednesday, Woden's day; Thursday, Thor's day; Friday, Friga (Scandinavian god); Saturday, Saturne's day.

subject: General Information
question: In a few words, tell what you know about the "long" parliament.
answer: It met in November, 1640. It attainted the Earl of Strafford; abolished the star chamber; the raising of tonnage and poundage without the consent of parliament was declared illegal, and it was provided that parliament could not be dissolved without its own consent. This parliament began and carried on the contest which culminated in the execution of Charles I, and the establishment of the commonwealth. The long parliament was dissolved by Cromwell in 1653.

subject: Arithmetic
question: Reduce 14 bushels 3 pecks to pints, and analyze in three ways.
answer: Bu. Pks. (a) 14 3 (b) 14.75 bu. X 4 X 8 X 2 = 944 pts. 4 59 8 472 2 944 pints. (c) 14¾ bu. X 4 X 8 X 2 = 944 pts. Note—The analysis is simply indicated. The pupil can expand it.

subject: Arithmetic
question: A surveyor stands at the point A. The first chain man stands due west of A 45 rods; the second chain man stands 60 rods due north of the first. What is the shortest distance from the surveyor to the second chain man?
answer: The distance from the surveyor = √40^2 + 60^2 = 78.7+ rods.

subject: Arithmetic
question: How many minutes will there be in the year 2000?
answer: In the year 2,000 there will be 366 days. 366 days X 24 X 60 = 527,040 minutes.

subject: Arithmetic
question: Subtract 7/8 of a leap year from a common year, and give the difference in hours, minutes, and seconds.
answer: 366 days X 24 = 8,784 hours = number of hours in a leap year. 8,784 of 7/8 = 7,686 hours. 8,760 - 7,686 = 1,074 hours.

subject: Arithmetic
question: The assessed valuation of A's farm and personal property is $900. He pays 2 per cent. school tax; 4 mills on the dollar township tax; 3 mills county tax; 2 mills state tax. Give the amount of his taxes.
answer: $900 X .02 = $18.00 = school tax. 900 X .004 = 3.60 = township tax. 900 X .003 = 2.70 = county tax. 900 X .002 = 1.80 = state tax. Total, $26.10.

subject: Language and Grammar
question: Make sentences in which the following words are used correctly: Impervious, homogeneous, imperturbable, incisive, poignant, mediocre, erudite, episode, epitome, equivocal.
answer: He is impervious to ridicule. The French are a homogeneous people. Under this torrent of abuse, he remained as imperturbable as a graven image. In an incisive speech he exposed the weakness of the party's policy. The loss of his brother caused him poignant sorrow. He is a man of mediocre abilities. He was a most erudite teacher. This was the most interesting episode in the narrative. We have in these few paragraphs an epitome of the entire work. He gave me an equivocal answer.

subject: Language and Grammar
question: Name five adjectives that cannot be compared.
answer: Circular, square, wooden, two, daily.

subject: Language and Grammar
question: Correct the following, and give reasons for each correction: "I am stopping at the hotel;" "In so far as that may be true;" "All of my classmates are going to the picnic."
answer: (a) I am staying at the hotel. Because stop is the reverse of start, and is incorrectly used. (b) So far as that may be true. In is superfluous. (c) All my classmates are going to the picnic. Of is unnecessary, and adds nothing to clearness of expression.

subject: Language and Grammar
question: Define tautology and pleonasm, and give an example of each.
answer: (a) It is a needless repetition of an idea in different words and phrases. Example: “Said party of the first part doth covenant and agree.” (b) The use of more words than are necessary to express an idea. Example: “He returned back again to his native state.”

subject: Language and Grammar
question: Define verse, distich or couplet, and triplet, and give an example of each.
answer: (a) A single line of poetry. Example: “Life is real, life is earnest.” (b) A couple of lines making complete sense. Example: “Know thyself, presume not God to scan; The proper study of mankind is man.” (c) Three lines rhyming together. Example: “As she fled fast through sun and shade, The happy winds upon her played, Blowing the ringlets from the braid.”

subject: Geography
question: How does the captain of a ship ascertain in what longitude and latitude his ship is at any time?
answer: (a) By comparing the time at Greenwich on the prime meridian with the noon in-stant where the ship is at that time, the longitude is computed. Every ship has a chronometer set to the time on the prime meridian. An allowance of 15 degrees is made for each hour. (b) The latitude is determined by measuring with a sextant the angle made by the sun with the horizon at noon.

subject: Geography
question: Give approximately the number of people belonging to each of the five races of mankind.
answer: Caucasian, about 600,000,000. Mongolian, about 600,000,000. Ethiopian, about 180,000,000. Malay, between 50,000,000 and 60,000,000. Indian, about 10,000,000.

subject: Geography
question: Tell briefly what is the work of the United States weather bureau.
answer: To make observations and records relating to the weather. Observation stations are maintained by the government in different parts of the United States. From these the condition of the temperature, appearance of the sky, pressure of the atmosphere, rain or snowfall, are telegraphed to Washington twice a day from the several observation points. From the bureau at Washington predictions concerning the weather, based upon the reports received, are sent by telegraph to central points in all parts of the country.

subject: Geography
question: Why is it that the western part of the Argentine Republic has but little moisture, while nearly all the western part of Brazil has a great deal?
answer: (a) In the temperate zone, in which zone the Argentine Republic is situated, the moisture is carried from the Pacific, but is condensed by the cold, western summits and slopes of the Andes, and flows back into the Pacific. Hence, the western part of the Argentine Republic, being east of the Andes, receives but little moisture. (b) In the torrid zone, the moisture is carried westward by the trade winds blowing from the Atlantic. The moisture is condensed on the eastern summits and slopes of the Andes; hence, western Brazil has a great deal of rain.

subject: Geography
question: Name the most important of the colonies or protectorates of France.
answer: Algeria, Tunis, New Caledonia, Anam, Madagascar.

subject: United States History
question: What is the origin of the term "corporal's guard" in United States history?
answer: A name given in derision to the few who supported President Tyler's policy from 1841 to 1845.

subject: United States History
question: He was a lawyer, governor of his state, minister to Great Britain, minister to Spain, candidate for President, member of congress, major general during a great war. Name him.
answer: Thomas Pinckney, South Carolina.

subject: United States History
question: What was the "Salary Grab"?
answer: The name given by the people to the act passed by Congress in 1873, increasing federal salaries. The President's salary was increased from $25,000 to $50,000; of the chief justice, from $8,500 to $10,000; of the associate justices, cabinet officers, vice president, and speaker of the house, from $8,000 to $10,000; of senators and representatives, from $5,000 to $7,500. What gave rise to the term "salary grab" was, that the act was made retroactive, and that the members of the Congress which passed the law were to receive the increase in salary from the beginning of their respective terms. In January, 1874, the act, except that part of it relating to the President and justices, was repealed.

subject: United States History
question: What is meant by "subsidies"? Give illustrations.
answer: Generally speaking, it means aid, in money, granted by the state to persons engaged in enterprises of a mercantile, manufacturing or industrial character. Specifically, it means money paid by a government to steamboat or railroad companies. The United States government has paid subsidies to lines from New York to Liverpool, Panama to Oregon, Charleston to Havana, and New York to Brazil.

subject: United States History
question: Name three states which were formed from parts of states already in existence.
answer: West Virginia, Kentucky, and Maine.

subject: General Information
question: Whence were the names of the months derived?
answer: From the Romans. January, Januaris, from Janus; February, Febru-aris, from Februa; March, Martius, from Mars; April, Aprilis, from Aperio; May, Maius, from Maia; June, Junius, from Juno; July, Julius, from Julius Cæsar; August, Augustus, from Augustus Cæsar; September means seventh month; October, eighth month; November, ninth month; December, tenth month. Originally, the Roman year began with March, and the number of months was 10.

subject: General Information
question: What is the difference between a privateer and a pirate?
answer: (a) A pirate is a ship which carries on rob-bery by force on the sea. (b) A privateer is an armed ship, owned by one or more private persons, to whom in time of war a government grants a commission authorizing the owner or owners and crew to capture the enemy's ships or other property, and to appro-priate to themselves, in whole or in part, the ships and goods seized.

subject: General Information
question: Name the author of each of the following quotations: "Well said; that was laid on with a trowel." "He makes no friend who never made a foe." "For he who is honest is noble, whatever his fortunes or birth."
answer: Shakespeare; Tennyson; Alice Cary.

subject: General Information
question: What changes have taken place in the government of Spain since 1800?
answer: In 1808, Napoleon drove out the reigning monarch and placed his own brother, Joseph Bona-parte, on the throne. In 1814, Frederick VII, the former monarch, was restored. In 1868, Queen Isabella was driven out of the country, and, in 1870 Amadeus Hohenzollern, second son of Victor Emanuel, accepted the crown, but he resigned a few years later. A republic was formed, and that gave place to the monarchy in 1874, when the crown was offered to Alfonso, son of the exiled queen. During the period from 1800 until the present (1895), there have been many rebellions in Spain; but few of them led to radical changes.

subject: General Information
question: Tell what you can concerning Corea and its people in 100 words?
answer: Corea is a kingdom of eastern Asia, occupying a peninsula bounded on the east by the Sea of Japan on the west by the Yellow sea; south, by the Strait of Corea; north, by Manchuria. Corea is a mountainous country, and is said to be rich in minerals. The temperature is more even than that of the Asiatic continent, but in the northern part it is very cold. The king has been a vassal of China, but within the limits of Corea his power has been absolute. The late war between Japan and China has made Corea an independent state.

subject: Arithmetic
question: What must be the length of each side of a cubical granary which is to hold 1,000 bushels of wheat?
answer: A bushel contains 1.25 cubic feet, nearly; 1,000 bushels contain 1,250 cubic feet, nearly. The cube root of 1,250 = 10.82 feet, nearly, length of each side of required granary.

subject: Arithmetic
question: The length of the school term in a certain school district was 6 months of 20 days each. One of the pupils was absent during the term 16 days, and he was tardy 15 minutes every day he was present. How much of the term did he lose?
answer: 20 × 6 = 120 days. 120 — 16 = 104 days. 15 × 104 = 1,560 minutes = 26 hours = 4⅓ school days. Total number of days lost, 108⅓.

subject: Arithmetic
question: The scale on a map is 8 miles to the inch. How many square miles are there in a county which is represented on the map by a rectangle 4½ inches long and 2¾ inches wide? How many acres are there in the county?
answer: 8 × 4.5 = 36 miles = length. 8 × 2.75 = 22 miles = breadth. 36 × 26 = 640 = 599,040 acres.

subject: Arithmetic
question: The fare on Kansas railroads is 3 cents a mile. Persons purchasing return tickets receive a discount of 10 per cent. If a return ticket costs me $7.56, what is the distance between the two stations?
answer: $7.56 = 90% of total fare; 7.56 ÷ 90 = .084, or 1% of total fare; .084 × 100 = $8.40, or total fare; 840 ÷ 3 = 280 miles.

subject: Arithmetic
question: What percentage of the area of Kansas are the combined areas of Maine, Vermont, New Hampshire, Massachusetts, Connecticut, Rhode Island, and New Jersey? (See any modern geography for areas.)
answer: Area of Kansas, 82,080 square miles. Combined area of Maine, Vermont, New Hampshire, Massachusetts, Connecticut, Rhode Island, and New Jersey, 73,030 square miles, The percentage is 88+.

subject: Language and Grammar
question: Make sentences in which the following words are used correctly: Excruciating, facile, versatile, exuberant, docile, inimical, irrefragable, metamorphosed, moribund.
answer: The pain he suffered was excruciating. He is a facile writer. He was a versatile politician. The foliage was exuberant. That is a docile child. The course he is pursuing is inimical to your interests. He proved his case by irrefragable testimony. That man seems to have been metamorphosed. The patient is moribund.

subject: Language and Grammar
question: Write the plural and the possessive singular of each of the following words: Court-martial, man-trap, cousin-german. Give the rules which are followed.
answer: Courts-martial, man-traps, cousins-german. In compound words, the part which describes the rest is generally the part pluralized. When a compound word is composed of a noun and an adjective, the sign of the plural should be added to the noun.

subject: Language and Grammar
question: Correct the following, and give reasons for each correction: "That book will do equally as well." "Had I have gone to school yesterday, I should have been first in my class." "I intend to pay my arrearages soon."
answer: That book will do equally well. As is superfluous. Had I gone to school yesterday, I should have been first in my class. Have is unnecessary, and adds nothing to the clearness of the expression. The third sentence appears to be grammatically correct, but in other respects it seems to be wrong; at any rate, the arrearages have not come.

subject: Language and Grammar
question: What is meant by "obscurity" in rhetoric? Give an example.
answer: A confusion in the expression of thought, usually because the thought itself is confused. Example: "The serene aspect of these writers, joined with the great encouragement I observe is given to another, or what is intended to be suspected, in which he indulges himself, confirmed me in the notion I have of the prevalence of ambition this way."

subject: Language and Grammar
question: What is alliteration? Give examples.
answer: The repetition of the same letter at the beginning of two or more words succeeding each other, or at short intervals. The recurrence of the same word in accented parts of words is also called alliteration. Example: Fly o'er waste fens and windy fields.—Tennyson. —International Dictionary.

subject: Geography
question: Where are the loftiest plateaus in the world?
answer: In Thibet. They are from 10,000 to 15,000 feet high.

subject: Geography
question: Where is the most foggy place in the world? Why is it so foggy there?
answer: On the Banks of Newfoundland. The vapor arising from the warm waters of the Gulf stream meets on the banks the cold current from the north. The vapor is condensed into fog continually.

subject: Geography
question: What are moraines and crevasses?
answer: (a) The rocks and debris brought down by avalanches to the edge of glaciers, and which form a dark band on each side. (b) Fissures in glaciers.

subject: Geography
question: Explain the flag signals of the United States weather bureau.
answer: White flag, clear or fair weather; blue flag, general rain or snow; half-white and half-blue flag, local rain or snow; white flag with a black square in the center, a cold wave; black triangular flag, change in temperature; temperature flag above fair-weather or rain flag, warmer weather; below them, colder weather; no temperature flag means continuation of present temperature.

subject: Geography
question: A certain country exports sugar, cotton, wool. Its capital is seven miles from the sea. The eastern part of the country is fertile; the western, arid. The form of government is republican. Name the country.
answer: Peru.

subject: United States History
question: What is meant by the "Amistad case"?
answer: On June 27, 1839, the L'Amistad was on her way from Havana to Puerto Principe with a cargo of slaves. The slaves mutinied, and killed all the crew except two, who were saved to navigate the ship to Africa. They, however, steered her in the direction of New York. She was seized by a United States warship — Calderon. The Spanish minister demanded the surrender of the slaves, on the ground that they were "property rescued from pirates." The abolitionists took the side of the slaves, and finally the supreme court decided that the slaves should be set free.

subject: United States History
question: What is the "Order of the Cincinnati"?
answer: It was founded by the officers of the revolutionary war, when the army was disbanded. Membership was limited to officers. The order is still in existence.

subject: United States History
question: When did the Hartford convention meet? What were its purposes?
answer: A convention of leading representatives of the federalists opposed to the war of 1812. It was composed of 27 members. Massachusetts, Connecticut, New Hampshire, Vermont and Rhode Island were represented. Resolutions recommending that the South be deprived of the representation given it for three-fifths of its slaves, that a two-thirds vote be required to admit a territory, that naturalized foreigners be debarred from holding offices or sitting in Congress, were passed.

subject: United States History
question: Who were the "Coodies"?
answer: A section of the federalists in New York city, in 1812, in favor of the war with England. The name was derived from the nom de plume—Abimalech Coody—of the leader, Gulian C. Verplanck.

subject: United States History
question: What was the "Covode investigation"?
answer: In the first session of the thirty-sixth congress, a committee, headed by Covode, of Pennsylvania, was appointed by the house of representatives to investigate charges of corruption made by two anti-Lecompton members against President Buchanan. The majority report was against the President.

subject: General Information
question: What countries were represented at the Paris monetary conference of 1867?
answer: Austria, Baden, Bavaria, Belgium, Denmark, the United States, France, Great Britain, Greece, Italy, The Netherlands, Portugal, Prussia, Russia, Sweden and Norway, Switzerland, Turkey, and Wurtemberg.

subject: General Information
question: What is meant by the "previous question" in parliamentary law?
answer: It is the name for a motion that debate cease at once, and that a vote be taken on the question under discussion.

subject: General Information
question: Name the author of each of the following quotations: "Heaven is above all yet; there sits a judge that no king can corrupt." "A man of pleasure is a man of pains." "Truth is truth howe'er it strike."
answer: Shakespeare, Young, Robert Browning.

subject: General Information
question: In what year was there a month which had but 19 days, and which had no new moon? Why?
answer: In 1752, the month of September had but 19 days, and had no new moon. The change from the old to the new style of dating had been made a short time before.

subject: General Information
question: Give the origin of the words clue, clumsy, coast, forestall, heathens.
answer: (a) From the custom of unwinding a ball or clew of thread when one went through a labyrinth, in order to find the way back. (b) In old English, clomsid meant hands frozen or stiffened, hence unable to grasp anything. (c) From the Latin costa, a rib or side. (d) From the custom of certain buyers, who would buy of the dealer while he was on his way to his stall in market. (e) "The word heathen acquired its meaning from the fact that, at the introduction of Christianity into Germany, the wild dwellers on the heaths longest resisted the truth."—Trench.

subject: Arithmetic
question: If a 124 ft. steeple casts a 93 ft. shadow, what is the height of a steeple that casts a 162 ft. shadow, under the same conditions?

subject: Arithmetic
question: Amount sent $2,530; commission, 2½ per cent.; find amount paid out.
answer: Amount sent, $2,530 = 102.5 per cent. of amount paid out. 2530 = $2,468.29+. Ans. 102.5

subject: Arithmetic
question: Reduce 1 gi. to the decimal of a gallon.
answer: 1 gi = 3 1/8 gal. = .03125 gal. Ans.

subject: Arithmetic
question: 5 seconds is what part of a degree?

subject: Arithmetic
question: When it is noon in Boston (71° 3' 30" W.), what time is it at San Francisco (122° 26' 15" W.)?
answer: Longitude of San Francisco=122° 26' 15" W. Longitude of Boston = 71° 3' 30" W. Difference in longitude = 51° 22' 45". Difference in time = 1/15 of dif. in longitude. 51° 22' 45" ——————— = 3hrs 25' 31". 15 Since Boston is east of San Francisco, noon at Boston gives forenoon at San Francisco = 3hrs 25' 31" before noon = 34' 29" after 8 o'clock A. M. Ans.

subject: Language and Grammar
question: What degree of comparison is expressed by each of the following: Better, ill, least, more? Write the three forms for each.
answer: Comparative, positive, superlative. Good, better, best; ill, worse, worst; little, less, least; much, more, most.

subject: Language and Grammar
question: What is an idiomatic phrase? Give three examples.
answer: A phrase having a peculiar sense not agreeing with the logical sense of its structural forms. I can make nothing of it. He is more than willing. I do observe you now, of late.

subject: Language and Grammar
question: Correct such of the following as are incorrect, and give reasons for so doing: (a) Will we ever be satisfied? (b) Should they be satisfied? (c) Would we be satisfied should we go? (d) You should be satisfied. (e) If I should be satisfied, would you be contented?
answer: (a) Shall we ever be satisfied? "Will" is never used interrogatively in the first person. (b) Would they be satisfied? In asking questions, the second and third person employ the auxiliary which is expected in reply. (c) Should we be satisfied, if we should go? "Would" and "should" are simply the past tenses of "shall" and "will," and follow the same rules. (d) You should be satisfied. Sometimes, as in this sentence, "should" expresses duty or obligation. (e) Correct. See (c) for reason.

subject: Language and Grammar
question: Write the past tense for the following verbs: Beware, may, must, shall, ought.
answer: “Beware” has no past. Might. “Must” has no past. Should. “Ought” has no past.

subject: Language and Grammar
question: Correct the following: (a) He did not pay over $4 for the hat. (b) He is a man of considerable talent. (c) Here are a couple of apples. (d) He graduated from the Kansas university yesterday. (e) Will you jeopardize your fortunes?
answer: (a) He did not pay more than $4 for the hat. (b) An idiom that is rapidly growing in favor, used for “superior ability.” (c) A vulgar expression for two. “Couple” has been used in this sense by Carlyle, Addison, Dickens, Sir P. Sidney, and others. (d) The authorities of the university do the graduating or measuring, the student is passive. Hence, “was graduated.” (e) Jeopard, instead of “jeopardize.”

subject: Geography
question: What country in the world leads in the production of iron, of steel, of coal?
answer: British Isles, United States, British Isles.

subject: Geography
question: Name in their order the five greatest wheat-producing countries of the earth.
answer: United States, France, Russia, Italy, Austro-Hungary.

subject: Geography
question: Name the three countries of the earth which have more than a half thousand inhabitants to each square mile of territory.
answer: Belgium, Egypt, Mauritius.

subject: Geography
question: Name the four countries (not governments) of the earth which support more than 50,000,000 people each.
answer: China proper, Russia, India, British India, United States.

subject: Geography
question: Name the three countries of the earth which have an area of more than 3,000,000 square miles.
answer: Brazil, Canadian Dominion, United States.

subject: United States History
question: What was the “kitchen cabinet”?
answer: President Jackson did not rely upon his first cabinet for advice; but he counseled with a few of his favorites, some of whom held subordinate positions in the departments. These men were called his “Kitchen Cabinet,” because of their inferior positions, and because through them the President could be influenced.

subject: United States History
question: What was the cause and what the result of the “Modoc war”?
answer: In 1872, the "Modocs," a band of Indians living in southern Oregon, to avenge some of the tribe murdered by white men 20 years before, and to force the United States government to consent to a more favorable treaty in their behalf, began hostilities. After a bloody war they were completely conquered and removed to the Indian Territory. There, many of the survivors have become civilized, many Christians, and one or two ministers of the gospel.

subject: United States History
question: What was the greatest Christmas gift ever received by a President of the United States while in office?
answer: Savannah, presented to President Lincoln, on Christmas day, 1865.

subject: United States History
question: What was the pan-American congress?
answer: A Congress of 66 members, from 17 independent American states, which held sessions in Washington, D. C., during the latter months of 1889 and the opening months of 1890, and made many recommendations, the principal one being that international difficulties should be settled by arbitration and not by war.

subject: United States History
question: What peculiar coincidences occurred in connection with the Washington and Bunker Hill monuments?
answer: Robert C. Winthrop, of Massachusetts, composed the oration at the laying of the cornerstone, and also that at the dedication of the Washington monument, 1848–1885. Daniel Webster, also of Massachusetts, performed the same services in connection with the Bunker Hill monument.

subject: General Information
question: What is meant by the phrase "tariff for revenue only," which occurs in United States political history?
answer: It means the levying of just enough tax on imports to meet the current expenses of the government.

subject: General Information
question: What schoolmaster in the time of Queen Elizabeth apologized for writing his books in Eng-lish?
answer: Roger Ascham.

subject: General Information
question: What is the origin of our word "mile"?
answer: It is corrupted from the Latin word mille, a thousand. The Roman mile was a thousand paces. Hence, the name "mile" from mille.

subject: General Information
question: Make a brief statement about the first re-corded sale of land.
answer: See Genesis, 23d chapter. Abraham bought a burial place for his family from Ephron, paying him 400 shekels of silver for it.

subject: General Information
question: We often see the expression casus belli in our newspapers. Explain its meaning.
answer: Casus belli is a Latin phrase which means, in law and diplomacy, some act or circumstance which renders war inevitable or unavoidable.

subject: Arithmetic
question: My watch gains 4 3/4 minutes a day. If it is exactly correct at noon on July 4, what time will it show at 3 P. M. on September 4?
answer: Total number of days, 62⅛. 4¾ minutes X 62⅛ = 295 minutes = 4 hours 55 minutes. Hence the watch at 3 P. M. on September 4 will show 7:55 P. M.

subject: Arithmetic
question: A train travels 33 3/4 miles an hour. Making no allowance for stops, how far will it travel between 10 1/2 minutes past 7 in the morning and 20 1/2 minutes past 3 in the afternoon?
answer: The number of hours from 10½ minutes past 7 A. M. until 20½ minutes past 3 P. M. is 8 hours and 10 minutes. 33¾ minutes X 8⅛ = 275⅝ minutes.

subject: Arithmetic
question: A farmer wishing to ascertain the number of acres in a rectangular field, and having no measure, “steps” the distances. He finds the length to be 200 steps, the width 160 steps. His steps average 3 1/4 feet. How many acres are in the field?
answer: The length = 650 feet; width, 520 feet. 650 X 520 ————— = 7.7+ acres. 43560

subject: Arithmetic
question: I have a gas jet which burns two feet of gas per hour. If I have it lighted 2 1/2 hours every evening from October 1 to April 1, inclusive, how much will my gas bill amount to at $1.50 per 1,000 feet?
answer: From October 1 to April 1, inclusive = 183 evenings. Gas burned each evening, 5 feet. 5 X 183 = 915. $1.50 X 915 = $1.37.

subject: Arithmetic
question: I bought a stock of goods for $4,500. The freight was 7 per cent. of the cost. I sell 35 per cent. of the goods at a profit of 33 1/3 per cent.; 45 per cent. at a profit of 25 per cent.; and the remainder at cost. What was my gain?

subject: Language and Grammar
question: Give the plural of each of the following words, with your reasons: Negro, grotto, Cato.
answer: Negroes, grottos, Catos, Nouns ending in o preceded by a consonant generally form their plural by adding es, Grotto is an exception to the rule. The plural of proper names ending in o is formed by adding s.

subject: Language and Grammar
question: Give two abstract nouns which do not add s to form their plurals. Give two abstract nouns which do add s to form the plural.
answer: (a) Meekness, decorum. (b) Affinities, gravities.

subject: Language and Grammar
question: Name five nouns which have the plural form but are used in both numbers.
answer: News, alms, odds, gallows, bellows.

subject: Language and Grammar
question: Correct the following, and give reasons: "I am not certain whether I can come." "He is the same man as came yesterday."
answer: (a) I am not certain whether I can come or not. The corresponding conjunction of whether is or, and the words or not should finish the sentence. (b) He is the same man that came yesterday. That should be used after same.

subject: Language and Grammar
question: Give an illustration of a proverb; of an epithet; of elision.
answer: (a) A sentence which briefly and forcibly expresses some practical truth. Example: "All is not gold that glitters." (b) An adjective expressing some attribute, quality or relation that is properly or specially appropriate to a person or thing, as a just man; a verdant lawn.—International Dictionary. (c) The suppression of a vowel or syllable; usually in poetry. Examples: " 'T is he;" "I'll ne'er forget."

subject: Geography
question: Define the terms low barometer and high barometer.
answer: The barometer is low when there is a great deal of vapor in the air, and the air is lighter than usual. A low barometer indicates a moist atmosphere. A high barometer indicates a heavy atmosphere, which may be due to denseness or dryness.

subject: Geography
question: What are volcanic islands? coral islands? Name two of each.
answer: They are formed by materials ejected by submarine volcanoes. Graham island, the Sandwich islands.

subject: Geography
question: At the equator, at what elevation is the snow line reached?
answer: Sixteen thousand feet,

subject: Geography
question: Starting from Manilla in a ship, and stopping on your way at Singapore, Cape Town, and Bahia, what islands and countries would you pass, and over what waters would you sail?
answer: (a) From the Pacific ocean into the China sea, through the Strait of Malacca, on the Indian ocean, across the South Atlantic ocean. China, Anam, Borneo, Sumatra, Siam, Ceylon, Mada- gascar, Cape Colony.

subject: Geography
question: Name the deepest fresh-water lake in America, and tell where it is situated.
answer: Crater lake, in Oregon. The average depth is 1,490 feet; deepest sounding, 1,196.

subject: United States History
question: What congress was in session 302 days?
answer: The thirty-first Congress.

subject: United States History
question: He was graduated at Princeton, was a lawyer, a United States senator, chancellor of a great university, president of a college, and was nominated for vice president of the United States. Name him.
answer: Theodore Frelinghuysen.

subject: United States History
question: What are political assessments? When and where did they originate?
answer: (a) Contributions exacted of candidates for office and office-holders by state, congressional, na- tional or municipal political committees, for the purpose of paying the expenses of campaigns. (b) The report of a committee to the twenty-fifth Congress states that a custom-house officer in New York was obliged to pay assessments. This is the first recorded case.

subject: United States History
question: Name all the places at which Congress held its meetings between the beginning of the revolution and the removal to Washington?
answer: Philadelphia, Baltimore, Lancaster, York, Princeton, Annapolis, Trenton, and New York.

subject: United States History
question: When were the most-noted commercial crises in United States history?

subject: General Information
question: Give the origin of each of the following words: Agate, ague, disparage, fulsome, neighbor.
answer: (a) Named after the place in which it was first found, the river Achates, in Sicily. (b) From the French aigue, meaning sharp. (c) The French word parage means equality of birth; so, to place a man below his proper station is to disparage him. (d) From the word foulsome. (e) From the two Anglo-Saxon words boor and nigh.

subject: General Information
question: Of two chapters in a certain book, one contains but 11 words, the other but 16. Name the book, and quote the contents of the two chapters.
answer: In a translation of Horrebow's "The Natural History of Iceland," one chapter is headed "Concerning Owls." The chapter is as follows: "There are no owls of any kind in the whole island." Another chapter, in the same book, "Concerning Snakes," reads thus: "No snakes of any kind are to be met with throughout the whole of the island."

subject: General Information
question: He was an eminent critic and essayist; he was imprisoned two years for attacking in a newspaper a royal personage; his father was at one time a lawyer in Philadelphia. Name him.
answer: Leigh Hunt.

subject: General Information
question: Name the author of each of the following quotations: “To know thyself—in others self concern; Wouldst thou know others? read thyself and learn.” “He that respects himself is safe from others; He wears a coat of mail that none can pierce.” “Silence more musical than any song.”
answer: (a) Schiller. (b) Longfellow. (c) Christina G. Rossetti.

subject: General Information
question: What was the origin of the interrogation and exclamation points?
answer: The exclamation point originally was I placed above O, thus, I/o. The interrogation point was formed by placing Q above O, thus, Q/o.
